query: What is the difference between inline, internal, and external CSS?

competitors.label: Getting started with CSS
competitors.selectionReason: This MDN syntax guide is the canonical official source that introduces the three primary ways of applying CSS to an HTML document.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on the basic CSS application modes and on the relationship between inline, internal, and external styles.
theirGaps: The guide is less interview-shaped and less explicit about maintainability, caching, and specificity side effects in real projects.

realWorldUseCases: Inline CSS is written directly on an element via the style attribute, internal CSS lives in a <style> tag in the <head>, and external CSS is loaded from a separate .css file via <link>. External styles are reusable and best for maintainability.
followUpConfusion:
Overview

CSS can be written directly inside HTML (inline), embedded within a <style> block (internal), or stored in a separate file (external). Understanding their differences is key to managing scalability, performance, and consistency in design.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the three application modes clearly, but it is lighter on the project-scale trade-offs around reuse, caching, and escalating specificity.
actionableExamples: It includes direct examples of the three CSS application methods, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the likely follow-up: external CSS wins for maintainability, while inline and internal styles come with scope and override trade-offs.

actionableExamples: Keep one compare-and-decide summary visible so the answer stays faster to scan than a broad intro guide.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical intro, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it turns the three CSS application modes into a concrete maintainability, reuse, and specificity decision.
